meta.name: Amit Davidi

categories.label: Chess
categories.title: Chess Engines & Visualizations
categories.description: Exploring classic board problems and building a full chess game from scratch, including advanced rules and position analysis.

projects.title: Knight's Tour — 50×50
projects.description: Visualization of the Knight's Tour problem solved on a massive 50×50 chess board, watching the knight traverse every square exactly once.
projects.longDescription:
## The Knight's Tour

The **Knight's Tour** is one of those classic problems that looks simple on paper: can a knight visit every square on a chess board exactly once?

On an 8×8 board you can solve it by hand. On a **50×50 board**, naive backtracking explodes — so I used **Warnsdorff's heuristic** (always move to the square with the fewest onward moves) to guide the search.

### What you're watching
The knight steps through all 2,500 squares, leaving a colored trail behind. Squares light up as they're visited, and the algorithm rarely needs to backtrack thanks to the heuristic.

### Takeaway
Greedy heuristics can turn an intractable search into something you can watch in real time.

projects.title: Full Chess Game
projects.description: A complete chess implementation featuring En Passant, Castling, and a full King Check detection mechanism.
projects.longDescription:
## A chess engine from scratch

Writing a chess game sounds straightforward until you hit the edge cases. This project implements **the full ruleset**:

- **Standard moves** for every piece
- **En Passant** — the pawn capture that trips up every beginner
- **Castling** (king-side and queen-side) with all legality checks
- **King check & checkmate detection** using legal-move generation
- **Promotion** when a pawn reaches the last rank

### Architecture
The board is represented as a 2D array of piece objects. Each piece knows how to generate its *pseudo-legal* moves; the game loop filters them against check rules to produce *legal* moves.

### What I learned
Rules interact in surprising ways — you can't castle *through* check, you can't castle *out of* check, and en passant is only legal on the immediate reply. Writing the test cases was more fun than writing the engine.

projects.title: PGN Parsing + Square Control
projects.description: A Magnus Carlsen game visualized through custom PGN parsing, with a square-control heatmap overlay.Red — abandoned squares with no attackers or defenders.Green — squares equally attacked and defended.
projects.longDescription:
## Watching a Magnus Carlsen game through a new lens

This project parses **PGN (Portable Game Notation)** files directly — no external libraries — and replays a real tournament game move by move, layering a **square-control heatmap** on top of the board.

### The heatmap
For every square, I count the number of attackers and defenders of each color:

- **Red squares** — nobody controls them; they're floating in no-man's-land
- **Green squares** — attack and defense are perfectly balanced
- **Colored gradient** — advantage goes to the stronger side

It's a cheap approximation of positional understanding, but even in a simple form it reveals why certain piece placements *feel* strong: they shift the control map.

categories.label: Pathfinding
categories.title: Path Finding Algorithms
categories.description: Classic pathfinding algorithms visualized across randomly generated mazes and weighted grids.

projects.title: Dijkstra on a Random Maze
projects.description: Dijkstra's algorithm discovering the shortest path through a procedurally generated maze, one cell at a time.
projects.longDescription:
## Dijkstra, visualized

Dijkstra's algorithm is the classical solution for finding the **shortest path** in a graph with non-negative edge weights. On a grid it's almost too easy — so I added a **procedurally generated maze** to make it interesting.

### Maze generation
The maze is carved using **recursive backtracking** — start anywhere, dig a tunnel, keep going until you dead-end, then backtrack. The result is a perfect maze (exactly one path between any two points).

### The search
Dijkstra explores outward from the start in concentric waves of increasing distance. The frontier glows as it expands; once it reaches the target, we trace the parent pointers backward to reveal the shortest path.

projects.title: Pathfinding Showcase
projects.description: A broader tour of pathfinding approaches with obstacles and weighted terrain.White — impassable barriers.Brown — weighted paths, like a traffic jam.
projects.longDescription:
## A tour of pathfinding algorithms

This is the extended showcase — multiple algorithms running on the same grid with obstacles and **weighted terrain**.

### The grid
- **White blocks** — impassable walls
- **Brown blocks** — weighted paths that cost extra to traverse (think traffic jam)
- **Start and goal** — user-placed

### Algorithms compared
- **Dijkstra** — optimal but doesn't know where it's going
- **A\*** — optimal *and* goal-directed (uses Manhattan distance heuristic)
- **Greedy Best-First** — fast but not always optimal

Watching Dijkstra flood uniformly while A* laser-focuses toward the goal is the best way to understand why heuristics matter.

categories.label: Fractals
categories.title: The Mandelbrot Set
categories.description: A deep dive into one of mathematics' most iconic fractals — rendered, colored, and explored.

projects.title: Mandelbrot Set Explorer
projects.description: Rendering and zooming into the infinite detail of the Mandelbrot set — where simple iteration produces stunning complexity.
projects.longDescription:
## The Mandelbrot Set

The definition is four lines of math: for each point `c` in the complex plane, iterate `z = z² + c` starting from `z = 0`. If `|z|` stays bounded forever, the point belongs to the set.

From that trivial rule comes one of the most visually rich objects in mathematics.

### What the renderer does
- Iterates each pixel until `|z| > 2` or the max iteration count is reached
- **Colors** pixels by how quickly they escape (fast escape = light, slow escape = dark)
- Supports **smooth zoom** — as you zoom in, new detail emerges that's never been rendered before

### The rabbit hole
Zoom deep enough and you find **miniature copies** of the full set buried inside itself. That self-similarity is the defining feature of a fractal.

categories.label: Computer Vision
categories.title: Computer Vision + Arduino
categories.description: A simple but genuinely fun experiment bridging software vision with physical hardware.

projects.title: Arduino Computer Vision
projects.description: Live computer vision driving an Arduino — simple in concept, but surprisingly satisfying to watch in action.
projects.longDescription:
## Webcam → Python → Arduino

The pitch is simple: your webcam sees something, Python decides what to do, and an Arduino *physically* reacts. Closing the loop between vision and hardware.

### The stack
- **OpenCV** for frame capture and basic detection (color tracking, motion, or face detection depending on the demo)
- **Python** bridge that converts detection → serial commands
- **Arduino** that reads the serial stream and drives an actuator (servo, LED, whatever's handy)

### Why it's fun
Software projects live on the screen. This one reaches into the room. Once you've got a servo turning to follow your face, you immediately want to connect it to everything.
